evidence_quotes.quote: they also effectively revoked my digital license by making the game no longer available for download even though I had previously purchased it.
digital vendors need to be forced to make all sold digital media available for download for as long as their companies exist.
(hidden) gambling mechanics as part of video games accessible to underage citizens.

paragraphs.text: This initiative seems very promising and covers very important subjects. The two most essential ones for me would be digital ownership for different types of media including movies, series, music, video games and many more, as well as (hidden) gambling mechanics as part of video games accessible to underage citizens. For both of these subjects the goal must be precise consumer protection laws that leave no room for unfair (and creative) ways to hurt consumer rights or children's mental health with these practices that are increasingly more common nowadays. In terms of digital ownership I can speak from my own experience. I bought the video game "The Crew" in the Ubisoft online store and when I thought I owned the game Ubisoft decided to both shut down the online servers without providing any option to continue playing the offline single player campaign included in the game, but they also effectively revoked my digital license by making the game no longer available for download even though I had previously purchased it. Considering how many games nowadays require an online connection to just be able to access the game even when most of the content does not rely on online functionality I am very worried how many games might be destroyed and become unaccessible even for those who purchased those games. A perfect example for potential future cases would be Gran Turismo 7 from Sony on the PlayStation 5 which also requires an online connection to access the single player campaign. Digital market places and publishers should not have the right to do any of this and should be forced to at least restore access to single player content inside of purchased games when they eventually end the continuous online support. Of course this should apply for other types of digital media as well. If you buy a physical CD you also own the music on it forever and a digital purchase should be no different and digital vendors need to be forced to make all sold digital media available for download for as long as their companies exist. When mentioning gambling mechanics I am primarily thinking of the typical lootbox style mechanics in games like EA Sports FC 25. Specifically the Football Ultimate Team game mode strongly incentivizes players to buy such lootbox packs to get a seemingly luck-based chance at getting the best players in the game for their personal team. This alone should qualify as gambling but if we take into account that the odds of actually having one of these players in one of the packs are ridiculously low this makes it very clear that this needs laws for much stronger regulations. Such lootbox mechanics harm the mental health and financial understanding of underage citizens drastically and that is why they should be classified as actual gambling. Here in Germany you do not have access to casinos until you turn 18 years old and I do not see why similar mechanics in video games should be treated any differently. I would be very happy if these topics would be explored in more detail and hopefully result in better consumer protection in modern digital market places.
